title: Geological foundations across Europe
dek: Marc Hesse began his geological training at the Technical University of Munich before completing his BSc in Geology at the University of Edinburgh.
narrative: Marc's journey into geosciences began with studies at the Technical University of Munich, where he developed an early interest in geological phenomena. He then moved to the University of Edinburgh, where he completed his BSc in Geology.

At Edinburgh, his work was recognized with multiple awards including the Edinburgh Geological Society Prize (2000), the Mineralogical Society Student Award (1999), and the Total Oil Marine Prize (1999). This period established his broad geological foundation before the pivot toward mathematical modeling and fluid dynamics.
proofPoints: BSc in Geology from University of Edinburgh (2000)
Edinburgh Geological Society Prize (2000)
Mineralogical Society Student Award (1999)
mapCaption: Geological training across Munich and Edinburgh.

title: From oceanography to applied mathematics
dek: MS in Oceanography from MIT-WHOI Joint Program, followed by MPhil in Fluid Flow from Cambridge University.
narrative: Marc's graduate education began at the MIT-WHOI Joint Program, where he earned an MS in Oceanography (2000–2002) with support from a Presidential Graduate Fellowship. This period marked his initial engagement with fluid dynamics in earth science contexts.

He then pursued an MPhil in Fluid Flow at the University of Cambridge's Department of Applied Mathematics and Theoretical Physics (2002–2003), supported by a European Trust Bursary and EPSRC Studentship. This Cambridge period represented a decisive pivot toward mathematical modeling and its applications in geosciences.
proofPoints: Presidential Graduate Fellowship at MIT (2000–2002)
MPhil in Fluid Flow from Cambridge DAMTP (2002–2003)
European Trust Bursary & EPSRC Studentship
mapCaption: Graduate training spanning MIT-WHOI and Cambridge University.

title: Doctoral specialization in porous media
dek: PhD in Petroleum Engineering from Stanford University, establishing expertise in porous media and fluid dynamics.
narrative: At Stanford University's Department of Energy Resources Engineering, Marc completed his PhD in Petroleum Engineering (approximately 2003–2008). This doctoral work established his core expertise in porous media flow, mathematical modeling, and numerical simulation.

The Stanford period solidified his interdisciplinary approach, combining rigorous applied mathematics with geological and engineering applications. This foundation would prove essential for his later work on CO2 sequestration, magma dynamics, and reactive transport.
proofPoints: PhD in Petroleum Engineering from Stanford University
Specialization in porous media and fluid dynamics
Foundation for computational geoscience career
mapCaption: Doctoral training at Stanford University in petroleum engineering and porous media.

title: Postdoctoral bridge to faculty career
dek: Postdoctoral researcher in tectonophysics at Brown University.
narrative: Following his Stanford doctorate, Marc served as a postdoctoral researcher in tectonophysics at Brown University (approximately 2008–2009). This position bridged his doctoral training and his faculty appointment at UT Austin.

The Brown postdoc expanded his research scope into solid earth geophysics and tectonophysics, complementing his fluid dynamics expertise and preparing him for the broad, interdisciplinary research program he would establish in Austin.
proofPoints: Postdoctoral researcher at Brown University
Research focus in tectonophysics
Bridge between Stanford PhD and UT Austin faculty position
mapCaption: Postdoctoral research at Brown University in tectonophysics.

title: Building a research program in computational geosciences
dek: From Assistant Professor to Dave P. Carlton Centennial Professor, establishing a leading research group in geological porous media.
narrative: In Fall 2009, Marc joined the Jackson School of Geosciences and the Institute for Computational Engineering and Sciences (now the Oden Institute) at the University of Texas at Austin as an Assistant Professor. His goal was to establish a leading research group in geological porous media and energy geosciences.

His teaching and research excellence were recognized early with the John A. and Katherine G. Jackson Centennial Teaching Fellowship (2011) and the Shell Companies Foundation Centennial Chair in Geophysics (2012). He was promoted through the ranks and currently holds the Dave P. Carlton Centennial Professorship in Geology.

At UT Austin, Marc has supervised numerous PhD students, taught innovative courses on continuum mechanics and numerical modeling, and built an internationally recognized research program spanning CO2 sequestration, magma dynamics, reactive transport, and planetary science.
proofPoints: Joined UT Austin as Assistant Professor in Fall 2009
Jackson Centennial Teaching Fellowship (2011)
Shell Companies Foundation Centennial Chair (2012)
Dave P. Carlton Centennial Professorship in Geology (current)
mapCaption: Academic career at UT Austin spanning 2009 to present.

title: Geological CO2 storage and natural analogues
dek: Pioneering research on CO2 dissolution dynamics using natural reservoirs like Bravo Dome as analogues for carbon sequestration.
narrative: Marc's research on geological CO2 storage has provided crucial insights into the long-term fate of carbon dioxide in subsurface reservoirs. His work on the Bravo Dome natural CO2 reservoir in New Mexico demonstrated that 366 MtCO2 (22% of the original 1.6 GtCO2) dissolved into brine over 1.2–1.4 million years.

This research, published in 2014 and featured on the U.S. Department of Energy website in 2018, established natural CO2 reservoirs as essential analogues for understanding the security and effectiveness of geological carbon storage. The work integrates geochemistry, geophysics, and reservoir engineering to constrain dissolution rates that are too slow to observe in pilot projects.
proofPoints: Bravo Dome study published (Sathaye et al. 2014)
Research featured by U.S. Department of Energy (2018)
Integration of geochemistry, geophysics, and reservoir engineering
mapCaption: CO2 sequestration research conducted at UT Austin with field sites across North America.

title: Icy ocean worlds and planetary habitability
dek: Current research on the habitability of icy moons like Europa and Enceladus, bridging geosciences and planetary science.
narrative: Marc's research has expanded into planetary science, focusing on the habitability of icy ocean worlds in the outer solar system. His work addresses the hydrothermal evolution of primordial planetesimals and the interpretation of ice-penetrating radar data for missions like Europa Clipper.

This research direction exemplifies his belief that fluid dynamics provides a unifying theme across the geosciences. By applying porous media theory and computational methods to icy moons, his group bridges classical solid-earth sciences with planetary exploration and astrobiology.
proofPoints: Research on Europa and Enceladus habitability
Hydrothermal evolution modeling of icy bodies
Collaboration with Europa Clipper mission team
mapCaption: Planetary science research at UT Austin with implications for outer solar system exploration.
